target.name: (-)-Elodeoidin A
target.motivation: Fascination with the structure of elodeoidins and the desire to establish a synthetic route, particularly using cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) reactions to streamline synthesis.

success_criteria.free_text: Convergent total synthesis of elodeoidins A and B, enabled by a cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) reaction between an aldehyde and an electron-deficient olefin. Elucidation of C8 stereochemistry for elodeoidin B.

key_disconnections.reasoning: To streamline the synthesis by forming key C-C bonds through two successive CDC reactions involving enedione 14 and aldehydes 13 and 15.

alternatives_considered.strategy: Baylis-Hillman reaction, Nozaki-Hiyama-Takai-Kishi coupling, Stetter reaction
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Unsuccessful, resulting in decomposition of substrates or no reactivity.

key_disconnections.reasoning: To access the final elodeoidins A and B from a common intermediate.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy leverages a novel cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) reaction to achieve convergence, aiming to overcome challenges associated with traditional C-C bond formation methods and to enable a more direct route to the complex natural products.

thought.background: Elodeoidins A-H (1-8) are a new family of natural products with a distinct five-membered beta-diketone moiety and a gem-dimethyl group. The isolation team proposed a retrobiosynthetic pathway.
thought.gap: A synthetic route to these natural products is needed.
thought.decision: To synthesize elodeoidins A and B, we envisioned a cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) reaction between an aldehyde and an electron-deficient olefin to streamline the synthesis.

evidence.text_span: Fascinated by the structure of elodeoidins, we sought to establish a synthetic route to these natural products. While biomimicry can guide the invention of novel synthetic strategies,11−17 in the case of elodeoidins A (1) and B (2), we envisioned that cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) reaction between an aldehyde and an electron-deficient olefin would significantly streamline the synthesis of the target molecules.

thought.background: Initial attempts to form the C-C bond between aldehyde 16 and enedione 14 or its derivatives 17 using classical methods.
thought.decision: To accomplish C-C bond formation, we first approached it using classical methods such as the Baylis−Hillman reaction, Nozaki−Hiyama−Takai−Kishi coupling, and Stetter reaction, expecting to form products 18, 19, or 20.

outcome.observation: All these approaches were unsuccessful, resulting in the decomposition of the substrates or complex mixtures.

evidence.text_span: We initiated our studies by exploring the C−C bond formation between aldehyde 16 and 2,2-dimethyl-4-cyclopentene-1,3-dione (14) or its derivatives 17. To accomplish this, we first approached it using classical methods such as the Baylis−Hillman reaction (Table 1, entry 1), Nozaki−Hiyama− Takai−Kishi coupling (Table 1, entry 2), and Stetter reaction (Table 1, entry 3). Unfortunately, all these approaches were unsuccessful, resulting in the decomposition of the substrates.

thought.background: Classical methods failed. Our group previously developed a visible-light-mediated Cu-catalyzed CDC reaction.
thought.gap: Direct coupling between isobutyraldehyde (16) and 2,2-dimethyl-4-cyclopentene-1,3-dione (14) did not show any reactivity.
thought.decision: To achieve C-C bond formation, we turned our attention to the visible-light-mediated Cu-catalyzed CDC reaction, expecting to form product 20.

outcome.observation: Direct coupling between isobutyraldehyde (16) and 2,2-dimethyl-4-cyclopentene-1,3-dione (14) using Cu-(dap)2Cl, TBEC, quinuclidine, and quinuclidine hydrochloride under 525 nm light irradiation did not show any reactivity.

evidence.text_span: We then turned our attention to the visible-light-mediated Cu-catalyzed CDC reaction previously developed by our group.24 Direct coupling between isobutyraldehyde (16) and 2,2-dimethyl-4-cyclopentene-1,3-dione (14) using Cu-(dap)2Cl, tert-butyl-peroxy 2-ethylhexylcarbonate (TBEC), quinuclidine, and quinuclidine hydrochloride under 525 nm light irradiation did not show any reactivity (Table 1, entry 4).

thought.background: Direct CDC reaction with enedione 14 failed. DFT calculations showed that the LUMO energy level of chlorinated enedione 17b was lower than that of enedione 14.
thought.gap: The enedione substrate needs to be more reactive.
thought.decision: To remedy the problem, the chloride moiety was introduced at the alpha-position of the enedione substrate, expecting to obtain product 21 in 70% yield.

outcome.observation: The CDC reaction between 16 and 17b under the aforementioned reaction conditions afforded the desired product 21 in 70% yield.

evidence.text_span: To remedy this problem, the chloride moiety was introduced at the α-position of the enedione substrate.27 DFT calculations revealed that the LUMO energy level of chlorinated enedione 17b was 0.262 eV lower than that of enedione 14 (see Supporting Information for details). To our pleasure, the CDC reaction between 16 and 17b under the aforementioned reaction conditions afforded the desired product 21 in 70% yield (Table 1, entry 5).

thought.background: Product 21 was obtained in good yield.
thought.gap: Derivatizing chloride 21 for subsequent C-C bond formation proved challenging.
thought.decision: To continue the synthesis, we attempted to derivatize chloride 21 for subsequent C-C bond formation.

outcome.observation: Derivatizing chloride 21 for subsequent C−C bond formation proved challenging (see Supporting Information for details).

evidence.text_span: However, derivatizing chloride 21 for subsequent C−C bond formation proved challenging (see Supporting Information for details).

thought.background: Derivatization of chlorinated product 21 was problematic. DFT calculation showed that the SOMO energy level of alpha,alpha-dialkoxy radical from 2-isopropyl-1,3-dioxolane (23) was higher than that of the acyl radical derived from 16.
thought.gap: An alternative coupling partner for the CDC reaction is needed.
thought.decision: To overcome the derivatization issue, we envisioned that the acetal derivative of 16 could serve as a plausible coupling partner for the CDC reaction, expecting to obtain product 22 in 48% yield.

outcome.observation: Delightfully, when dioxolane 23 and enedione 14 were subjected to the Cu(dap)2Cl.-catalyzed CDC reaction conditions, product 22 was obtained in 48% yield (Table 1, entry 6).

evidence.text_span: Alternatively, we envisioned that the acetal derivative of 16 could serve as a plausible coupling partner for the CDC reaction. DFT calculation showed that the SOMO energy level of α,α-dialkoxy radical from 2-isopropyl-1,3-dioxolane (23) was 1.021 eV higher than that of the acyl radical derived from 16 (see the Supporting Information for details). Delightfully, when dioxolane 23 and enedione 14 were subjected to the Cu(dap)2Cl.-catalyzed CDC reaction conditions, product 22 was obtained in 48% yield (Table 1, entry 6).

thought.background: The Yamano group previously synthesized aldehyde 24 in six steps from geraniol.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize dioxolane 27, a plausible CDC partner, from aldehyde 24.
thought.decision: To synthesize dioxolane 27, we commenced from aldehyde 24, expecting to obtain terminal olefin 25 in 98% yield via Wittig olefination.

outcome.observation: Wittig olefination of 24 with the ylide produced from methyltriphenylphosphonium bromide and potassium tert-butoxide resulted in terminal olefin 25 in 98% yield.

evidence.text_span: The synthesis of dioxolane 27 commenced from aldehyde 24, previously synthesized by the Yamano group in six steps from geraniol (Scheme 2).28 Wittig olefination of 24 with the ylide produced from methyltriphenylphosphonium bromide and potassium tert-butoxide resulted in terminal olefin 25 in 98% yield.

thought.gap: Need to convert terminal olefin 25 to primary alcohol 26.
thought.decision: To synthesize primary alcohol 26, we performed regioselective hydroboration with dicyclohexylborane and subsequent oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, expecting 83% yield.

outcome.observation: Regioselective hydroboration with dicyclohexylborane and subsequent oxidation with hydrogen peroxide yielded primary alcohol 26 in 83% yield.

evidence.text_span: Regioselective hydroboration with dicyclohexylborane and subsequent oxidation with hydrogen peroxide yielded primary alcohol 26 in 83% yield.

thought.gap: Need to convert primary alcohol 26 to aldehyde 13.
thought.decision: To synthesize aldehyde 13, we oxidized the primary alcohol moiety of 26 using Dess-Martin periodinane, expecting 79% yield.

outcome.observation: Dess−Martin periodinane oxidized the primary alcohol moiety of 26 to furnish aldehyde 13 in 79% yield.

evidence.text_span: Dess−Martin periodinane oxidized the primary alcohol moiety of 26 to furnish aldehyde 13 in 79% yield.

thought.gap: Need to transform aldehyde 13 to dioxolane 27.
thought.decision: To synthesize dioxolane 27, we performed acetalization of aldehyde 13 in the presence of 1,2-bis(trimethylsiloxy)ethane and trimethylsilyl triflate, expecting 65% yield.

outcome.observation: The transformation of aldehyde 13 to dioxolane 27 was achieved in the presence of 1,2-bis(trimethylsiloxy)ethane and trimethylsilyl triflate in 65% yield.

evidence.text_span: The transformation of aldehyde 13 to dioxolane 27 was achieved in the presence of 1,2-bis(trimethylsiloxy)ethane and trimethylsilyl triflate in 65% yield.29

thought.gap: Need to perform the key CDC reaction between dioxolane 27 and enedione 14.
thought.decision: To synthesize C2-C3 coupled product 28, we allowed dioxolane 27 and enedione 14 to react with Cu(dap)2Cl, TBEC, and quinuclidine in acetonitrile under green light irradiation, expecting 45% yield.

outcome.observation: When dioxolane 27 and enedione 14 were allowed to react with Cu(dap)2Cl, TBEC, and quinuclidine in acetonitrile under green light irradiation (525 nm), the C2−C3 coupled product 28 was obtained in 45% yield (Scheme 2).

evidence.text_span: With solid access to dioxolane 27, we next investigated the key CDC reaction. When dioxolane 27 and enedione 14 were allowed to react with Cu(dap)2Cl, TBEC, and quinuclidine in acetonitrile under green light irradiation (525 nm), the C2−C3 coupled product 28 was obtained in 45% yield (Scheme 2).

thought.gap: Need to perform the next CDC reaction to form the C1-C7 coupled product 29.
thought.decision: To synthesize C1-C7 coupled product 29, we performed conjugation between 28 and isobutyraldehyde (16) under the established CDC reaction conditions, expecting 43% yield.

outcome.observation: Subsequent conjugation between 28 and isobutyraldehyde (16) cultivated the C1−C7 coupled product 29 in 43% yield under the established CDC reaction conditions.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent conjugation between 28 and isobutyraldehyde (16) cultivated the C1−C7 coupled product 29 in 43% yield under the established CDC reaction conditions.24 Compound 29 consists of all C−C bonds present in elodeoidin A (1).

thought.background: Compound 29, containing all C-C bonds of elodeoidin A, was obtained. The remaining steps were global deprotection of acetals and in situ hemiketalization.
thought.gap: Methanolysis or hydrolysis of acetal 29 was unsuccessful.
thought.decision: To complete the synthesis, we attempted global deprotection of acetals and in situ hemiketalization of 29 using various Brønsted and Lewis acids.

outcome.observation: Despite numerous attempts using various Brønsted and Lewis acids, the methanolysis or hydrolysis of acetal 29 was unsuccessful, with the acetal moiety remained intact in most cases. Efforts to deprotect the acetal moiety of compound 28 also proved problematic.

evidence.text_span: Compound 29 consists of all C−C bonds present in elodeoidin A (1). The remaining steps were the global deprotection of acetals and in situ hemiketalization. However, despite numerous attempts using various Brønsted and Lewis acids, the methanolysis or hydrolysis of acetal 29 was unsuccessful, with the acetal moiety remained intact in most cases. Efforts to deprotect the acetal moiety of compound 28 also proved problematic (see Supporting Information for details on the deprotection conditions tested for 28 and 29).

thought.background: Acid-mediated hydrolysis of 28 and 29 proved problematic.
thought.gap: An alternative approach for the acylation of enedione 14 with the aldehyde substrate is needed.
thought.decision: To overcome the deprotection issues, we envisioned a sequence involving radical-mediated hydroacylation and subsequent oxidation, expecting to obtain conjugated intermediate 31 in its enol form.

outcome.observation: Gratifyingly, when enedione 14 was allowed to react with isobutyraldehyde (16) in the presence of tetrabutylammonium decatungstate (TBADT) under 370 nm light irradiation, conjugated intermediate 31 was obtained in its enol form.

evidence.text_span: As an alternative approach for the acylation of enedione 14 with the aldehyde substrate, we envisioned a sequence involving radical-mediated hydroacylation and subsequent oxidation. Gratifyingly, when enedione 14 was allowed to react with isobutyraldehyde (16) in the presence of tetrabutylammonium decatungstate (TBADT) under 370 nm light irradiation,30,31 conjugated intermediate 31 was obtained in its enol form (Scheme 3).

thought.background: Conjugated intermediate 31 was obtained in its enol form.
thought.decision: To synthesize enetrione 20, we treated 31 with phenylselenyl chloride and pyridine, followed by oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, expecting 68% yield over 2 steps.

outcome.observation: Subsequent treatment of 31 with phenylselenyl chloride and pyridine, followed by oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, afforded enetrione 20 in 68% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent treatment of 31 with phenylselenyl chloride and pyridine, followed by oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, afforded enetrione 20 in 68% yield over 2 steps.

thought.background: Enetrione 20 was obtained. We attempted the next acylation reaction using the previously described CDC reaction conditions.
thought.gap: Initial attempts of CDC reaction between enetrione 20 and aldehyde 13 did not yield the desired product.
thought.decision: To synthesize product 12, we attempted the CDC reaction between enetrione 20 and aldehyde 13 under the standard reaction conditions, expecting to obtain the desired product.

outcome.observation: However, initial attempts of CDC reaction between enetrione 20 and aldehyde 13 under the aforementioned standard reaction conditions did not yield the desired product.

evidence.text_span: We then attempted the next acylation reaction using the previously described CDC reaction. However, initial attempts of CDC reaction between enetrione 20 and aldehyde 13 under the aforementioned standard reaction conditions24 did not yield the desired product (Scheme 3).

thought.background: Initial CDC reaction between enetrione 20 and aldehyde 13 failed. The highly electrophilic enetrione 20 was incompatible with nucleophilic quinuclidine.
thought.gap: A modified CDC reaction condition is needed.
thought.decision: To achieve the desired coupling, we discovered that the desired product 12 was formed in the absence of quinuclidine and its hydrochloride salt, and further optimized the conditions using Cu(dap)2PF6 and TBPB, expecting 49% yield.

outcome.observation: After extensive experimentations, we discovered that the desired product 12 was formed in the absence of quinuclidine and its hydrochloride salt (24% yield). Further optimization studies revealed that Cu(dap)2PF6 and tert-butyl peroxybenzoate (TBPB) are the optimal catalyst and oxidant, respectively, for this transformation, providing the coupled product 12 in 49% yield.

evidence.text_span: We noticed that the highly electrophilic enetrione 20 was incompatible with nucleophilic quinuclidine. After extensive experimentations, we discovered that the desired product 12 was formed in the absence of quinuclidine and its hydrochloride salt (24% yield). Further optimization studies revealed that Cu(dap)2PF6 and tert-butyl peroxybenzoate (TBPB) are the optimal catalyst and oxidant, respectively, for this transformation, providing the coupled product 12 in 49% yield.

thought.gap: Need to perform methanolysis and hemiketalization to obtain (-)-elodeoidin A (1).
thought.decision: To synthesize (-)-elodeoidin A (1), we performed methanolysis of the acetonide moiety of 12 and hemiketalization of the resulting alcohol intermediate in the presence of a catalytic amount of acetyl chloride in methanol, expecting 44% yield.

outcome.observation: Final methanolysis of the acetonide moiety of 12 and hemiketalization of the resulting alcohol intermediate in the presence of a catalytic amount of acetyl chloride in methanol furnished the first synthetic sample of (−)-elodeoidin A (1) in 44% yield (14% overall yield from 24 over 5 steps; 7.2% overall yield from geraniol over 11 steps).

evidence.text_span: Final methanolysis of the acetonide moiety of 12 and hemiketalization of the resulting alcohol intermediate in the presence of a catalytic amount of acetyl chloride in methanol furnished the first synthetic sample of (−)-elodeoidin A (1) in 44% yield (14% overall yield from 24 over 5 steps; 7.2% overall yield from geraniol over 11 steps).

thought.background: The total synthesis of elodeoidin A was achieved. The mechanism of the newly discovered CDC reaction was investigated.
thought.gap: Need to complete the total synthesis of elodeoidin B (2) and determine the configuration at the C8 stereocenter.
thought.decision: To synthesize both structural candidates of elodeoidin B, 2a and 2b, we employed the analogous synthetic route presented in Scheme 3, starting from (S)-2-methylbutanal (45) and (R)-2-methylbutanal (46), respectively.

outcome.observation: By employing the analogous synthetic route presented in Scheme 3, we synthesized both structural candidates of elodeoidin B, 2a and 2b, from (S)-2- methylbutanal (45) and (R)-2-methylbutanal (46), respectively (Scheme 7).

evidence.text_span: We then aimed to complete the total synthesis of elodeoidin B (2) and determine the configuration at the C8 stereocenter. While DFT-based predictions of NMR chemical shifts and coupling constants are effective for conformationally rigid molecules,48−50 t he conformational flexibility of both structural candidates, 2a and 2b, made such calculations particularly challenging, leaving total synthesis of the target compound as the only way to elucidate the undetermined C8 stereochemistry. By employing the analogous synthetic route presented in Scheme 3, we synthesized both structural candidates of elodeoidin B, 2a and 2b, from (S)-2- methylbutanal (45) and (R)-2-methylbutanal (46), respectively (Scheme 7).

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield from geraniol over 11 steps for (-)-elodeoidin A

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Longest linear sequence from geraniol to (-)-elodeoidin A

validation.significance: Achieved a convergent total synthesis of elodeoidins A and B using a novel CDC reaction. The mechanism of the CDC reaction was elucidated through DFT calculations, revealing a concerted deprotonation within a transient formal Cu(III) complex. The total synthesis of both structural candidates of elodeoidin B demonstrated that natural elodeoidin B exists as a mixture of diastereomers at the C8 stereocenter.

source.title: Total Synthesis of (-)-Elodeoidins A and B.
source.authors: Lee C, Kang G, You J, Kim T, Lee HS, Park Y, Han S.
